register: Private aviation operators — plain English, no insider jargon. NEVER use: 'Part 135', 'ARGUS', 'Wyvern', 'fractional ownership', 'jet card', 'empty-leg', 'flight department KPI'. Replace with: 'private jet charter company', 'people who fly private', 'private flight shopper', 'inquiry form', 'quote request'. Tone: discreet, surgical, neutral. No hype, no emoji, no high-pressure. Jeremy Miner NEPQ + PAS structure.

A.name: Pattern Interrupt + Soft Disarm
A.desc: NEPQ + PAS. Best for never-engaged private jet charter operators with verified phone. Permission-based open, surgical reveal, two leaks + withheld third. ~150s.

beats.title: Disarm + Permission
beats.line: I know this is out of the blue. You're with {company}, correct? Every quarter we do a quiet review of private aviation websites — looking at what a quote shopper actually sees. Got 60 seconds, or did I catch you bad?
beats.followup: Fair enough — I'll be quick. 60 seconds and you decide if it's worth more.
beats.note: Miner low-status open. Pause after 'quote shopper actually sees.' Don't fill silence. High-net-worth operators reward composure.

beats.title: Surface the Buyer's Reality
beats.line: When someone who flies private lands on your site, there's about an 8-second window where they decide you're serious — or you're not quite there yet. Most operators lose them in that window. Not price, not the aircraft. Three structural things on the page.
beats.followup: Most operators say that — and the page usually looks fine. The leak is rarely visual. It's structural.
beats.note: PAS — Problem. The 8-second window is universally true in luxury commerce. Don't sell hard yet.

beats.title: Two Leaks Revealed, Third Withheld
beats.line: On {company} specifically, two things jumped out. First — {leak_1}. Second — {leak_2}. Neither is fatal alone. But on flights booking at $25,000 to $80,000, even a small drop in inquiry-to-booking is real money walking off the page weekly. There's a third leak that costs more than both — I'd rather walk you through it on a 15-minute screen share than rush it on the phone.
beats.followup: The third one's the easiest to fix and the most expensive to ignore. Worth 15 minutes.
beats.note: DO NOT reveal leak 3 on the phone. The third leak IS the meeting. If they push: 'I'd rather earn the 15 minutes than spoil it. Thursday or Friday?'

beats.title: Why Us + Discretion
beats.line: Quick on us — we work only with private aviation. Charter companies, jet card programs, shared-ownership. No leisure, no commercial. And we're discreet — no LinkedIn posts, no case studies, no name-drops. Same discretion you'd want for your own clients.
responses: Who else have you worked with?
beats.followup: References after the review call if it's the right fit on both sides.
beats.note: Plain English only. NEVER name-drop specific operators — discretion IS the product.

beats.title: Book the Time
beats.line: Two options. Either I send the written review — 8 pages with screenshots, normally $4,500, yours to keep — or 15 minutes with {strategist} on screen share. Thursday at 10 Eastern or Friday at 2 Eastern?
beats.followup: Perfect — best email for the calendar invite? Let me read it back to make sure I have it right.
beats.note: Doc-only operators convert ~22% on second touch — log as 'doc-only follow-up.' Always read email back verbatim — wrong email is the #1 no-show cause.

B.name: Curiosity Hook + Sandler Negative Reverse
B.desc: NEPQ Belief-Building + Sandler. Best for re-engagement, referrals, or operators who ghosted on a prior touch. Lowers the bar by telling them you're easy to say no to. ~135s.

beats.title: Curiosity Hook
beats.line: Hi — I work with private jet charter operators on something that quietly costs most of them about 30% of the inquiries they're paying to generate. 60 seconds to tell you what it is? If it doesn't apply to {company}, I'll let you go.
beats.followup: Not selling anything on this call. Just telling you what I see on your site — you decide if it's worth more.
beats.note: If they say 'what is it?' — you've won the open. Slow down. Composure signals you're not a typical agency cold-caller.

beats.title: Build the Belief
beats.line: When someone with the means to fly private lands on a charter site, there's an 8-second window where they decide you're serious — or not. Most operators lose them around the 6-second mark. Not price, not the airplanes. Three structural things on the page.
beats.followup: I'll show you exactly what's happening on your page in a minute.
beats.note: Belief-building. Plain English replaces all aviation jargon.

beats.title: Two Leaks Revealed, Third Withheld
beats.line: I pulled up {company} this morning. {leak_1} is one. {leak_2} is the second. The third one costs more than both, and it's the easiest to fix — but I'd rather show it to you on a screen share than rush it now.
beats.followup: Thursday or Friday — I promise it's worth 15 minutes. Worst case, you've still got two free fixes from this call.
beats.note: Hold the third leak. If they push: 'I'd rather earn the time than spoil it. Thursday or Friday?'

beats.title: Lower the Stakes (Sandler Negative Reverse)
beats.line: Look — not a sales call. {strategist} walks you through what we'd change and in what order. Then you either do it yourself, hand it to your in-house team, or — if it's the right fit on both sides — we do it for you. Most operators take the document and run. That's fine.
beats.followup: No catch. {strategist} is running a handful personally this quarter. And we don't publish names — truly anonymous.
beats.note: Sandler Negative Reverse: telling them you're EASY to say no to lowers resistance. High-net-worth operators hate being sold to.

beats.title: Close on Time
beats.line: Thursday at 10 Eastern or Friday at 2 Eastern — which works better? {strategist} blocks it himself, so whichever you pick is locked.
beats.followup: Perfect — best email for the invite? Let me read it back.
beats.note: Two specific slots, not 'whenever works.' High-net-worth prospects respect bounded calendars. Read email back.

C.name: Paid-Search Leak + The Math
C.desc: PAS + Specificity Cluster + Loss-Frame. Best for operators visibly running paid search (verify on Google before dialing). Math-led — anchors on what they're paying per click. ~135s.

beats.title: Paid-Search Anchor
beats.line: Quick reason for the call — I see {company} is running paid search on '{primary_kw}'. Clicks in your category are running ${cpc_low} to ${cpc_high} each. Got 60 seconds?
beats.followup: Not about the ads — the ads are doing their job. The leak is on what happens after the click.
beats.note: PRE-CALL: verify they actually run ads by Googling their primary keyword from {market}. If you don't see them on the SERP, switch to Variant A or B.

beats.title: The Math, Fast
beats.line: Fast math — if your click costs ${cpc_high} and 1.5 out of 100 clicks fills out a quote form, you're paying low thousands per real inquiry. Move that page from 1.5% to 3% — well inside normal range for a properly built private aviation site — and you cut cost-per-quote in half. Same spend, double the quotes.
beats.followup: Higher than 3% or higher than 1.5%? Big difference. Most operators we review land between 0.8% and 2.2%.
beats.note: Don't argue numbers. Anchor on the LIFT, not the current state. If they claim 5%+, ask: 'Measured at quote-submit, or at booked-flight?'

beats.title: Where the Spend Is Leaking
beats.line: I pulled up where your ad spend is landing. Two things jumped out — {leak_1}, and {leak_2}. Both are afternoon fixes for a developer. The third leak is the most expensive of the three — that one I'd rather walk you through on a screen share with the page open.
beats.followup: The review itself is free. The fix depends on what we find — some operators do it in-house once they see the diagnosis.
beats.note: 'Afternoon fix' makes the win feel close. Third-leak withhold creates the meeting pull.

beats.title: Why Us + Discretion
beats.line: Quick on us — we work only with private aviation. Charter, jet card, shared-ownership. No leisure, no commercial. We're discreet — names stay between us and the principal. Same discretion you'd want for your own clients.
beats.followup: References after the review call if it's the right fit on both sides.
beats.note: Plain English only. NEVER name-drop on a cold call.

beats.title: Close the Time
beats.line: Two options. Either I send the written review — 8 pages with screenshots, normally $4,500, yours to keep — or 15 minutes with {strategist} on screen share. Thursday at 10 Eastern or Friday at 2 Eastern?
beats.followup: Great — best email? And anyone else from your side who should be on it — usually whoever owns the website.
beats.note: Read email back verbatim. Wrong email kills show-up rate. Confirm second attendee.
